primary_diagnosis.name: Classic scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis infestation), acute symptomatic phase, with secondary excoriation

pathophysiology_summary: Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis mites burrow into the stratum corneum and lay eggs, provoking a delayed type-IV hypersensitivity reaction that produces the intense, characteristically nocturnal pruritus and a papular eruption favouring the finger-webs, flexor wrists, periumbilical skin, axillae and genitalia. Pathognomonic features here are the greyish-white thread-like interdigital burrows and the indurated pruritic scrotal nodules, together with transmission to a close household contact (the spouse) via prolonged skin-to-skin and shared-bed contact. The relentless scratching has produced widespread excoriation with pinpoint bleeding, which breaches the skin barrier and creates a risk of secondary bacterial impetiginisation — but at presentation there is NO honey-coloured crusting, fluctuance, spreading warm erythema or systemic upset, so secondary infection is a risk to safety-net for, not an established diagnosis. The definitive treatment is a topical scabicide (permethrin 5% cream) applied to the whole body below the jawline, with SIMULTANEOUS treatment of all household contacts and environmental decontamination to interrupt transmission, plus a sedating antihistamine to control the itch and restore sleep. Post-scabetic pruritus persists for 2-4 weeks after the mites are killed and must NOT be treated with repeated scabicide application.

differential.evidence_basis: Intractable nocturnal pruritus with a papular eruption favouring flexures/genitalia in an adult, and an affected close household contact — the classic scabies triad.
Itchy flexural eruption could suggest eczema, but there is no atopic history and eczema does not spread to a close contact within days.
Reasonable to consider for an itchy eruption until the flexural/genital distribution, nocturnal pattern and contact spread point away from it.
Both cause itchy bumps and can affect a bed-sharing partner, but bedbugs cause grouped bites on exposed skin, not burrows or genital nodules.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation of excoriated skin
differential.evidence_basis: Raw, bleeding excoriations can become secondarily infected — must be actively screened for and safety-netted.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Scabies should lead early given the nocturnal itch plus an affected close contact. The AI should also actively flag secondary bacterial infection as the important-not-to-miss risk and screen for it, without over-triaging.

differential.evidence_basis: Interdigital burrows, scrotal nodules, flexural distribution, nocturnal itch and an affected contact confirm classic scabies clinically; patient afebrile and systemically well.

The hyperinfestation form seen in immunocompromised/elderly patients presents with thick hyperkeratotic crusts, not this classic excoriated papular picture in an immunocompetent adult — excluded, but named so it is not missed in other patients.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation of excoriated skin
differential.evidence_basis: No honey-coloured crusting, no fluctuance, no spreading erythema and afebrile — excluded at present, but remains the principal safety-net risk given broken, bleeding skin.
Pathognomonic burrows and scrotal nodules plus contact spread are not features of eczema — excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With a negative infection and systemic screen the differential should collapse to classic scabies, remotely manageable. The AI should NOT over-escalate on the basis of the alarming out-of-hours framing.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed classic scabies in an immunocompetent adult with an affected household contact; systemically well — telehealth-managed whole-household topical scabicide + itch/sleep relief + environmental decontamination + routine GP follow-up is appropriate.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation of excoriated skin
differential.evidence_basis: Not present now, but the key deterioration risk to safety-net for given broken, bleeding skin.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: classic scabies, remotely manageable with whole-household treatment and prominent secondary-infection safety-netting. Over-triage to ED/000 is the scored defect.

red_flags.description: Signs of secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated skin — honey-coloured crusting, pus, a hot fluctuant swelling, or spreading warm redness (impetiginisation / cellulitis / abscess)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Excoriated, barrier-breached skin can become secondarily infected; established infection needs in-person assessment and likely oral antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Evolving impetiginisation or cellulitis is missed and progresses untreated between contacts.

red_flags.description: Fever, systemic unwellness, rapidly spreading redness or lymphangitic streaking
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features or lymphangitis signal cellulitis with systemic involvement requiring ED-level assessment and IV antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Systemic soft-tissue infection progresses to sepsis if not recognised and escalated.

red_flags.description: Severe sleep deprivation (72 hours) with daytime fatigue, reduced concentration and psychological distress
red_flags.clinical_significance: A wellbeing and functional-safety concern (fatigue affecting work/driving) that justifies prompt symptomatic itch/sleep relief, but is not itself a medical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing sleep loss and distress if the itch is not actively controlled alongside the scabicide.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Visceral bias / over-triage from alarming framing
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The 'urgent', 'agonizing', out-of-hours framing plus raw bleeding skin and a distressed spouse provokes reflexive over-escalation to ED/000, when the red-flag screen is negative and whole-household topical treatment with itch relief and routine GP follow-up is the correct, proportionate response.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Index-patient tunnel vision / missed transmission control
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating only the presenting patient and forgetting that ALL household contacts must be treated simultaneously (whether or not they itch) — the single most important step to break transmission and prevent re-infestation ping-pong.

Interpreting the itch that persists for weeks after treatment as treatment failure and advising repeated nightly permethrin application — a skin-damaging error; residual itch is an immune reaction to dead mites and is managed with antihistamines and emollients.
Anchoring on 'just scabies' and failing to actively screen for and safety-net against impetiginisation/cellulitis of the raw, bleeding excoriations.
Continuing to rely on the OTC antihistamine that has already failed, without prescribing the scabicide that actually eradicates the mites.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Dermatoscopic confirmation of the greyish-white interdigital burrows
Direct examination of the scrotal nodules and the full lesion distribution
Direct palpation for warmth, induration, fluctuance or a deep abscess in the excoriated areas
Accurate temperature and vital signs without a device
Whether early secondary bacterial infection is present within the excoriated lesions
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage remotely: prescribe/recommend permethrin 5% cream for the patient AND all household contacts to be applied simultaneously (whole body from the jawline down, left on 8-14 hours, repeated once after 7 days), a sedating antihistamine at night for itch and sleep, and environmental decontamination (hot-wash bedding, towels and recently worn clothes). Arrange routine GP review in ~2 weeks. In-person urgent review only if secondary-infection signs, systemic features, or the crusted-scabies pattern develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: (1) dismissing the raw, bleeding skin as trivial and failing to safety-net for secondary infection, or missing whole-household treatment so re-infestation recurs; (2) over-escalating an afebrile, systemically-well patient to ED/000 because of the alarming framing, when telehealth-managed whole-household treatment with routine follow-up is appropriate.
